reverification_note: The committed probe.py is the exact file executed on 2026-07-17. It was re-run end-to-end on 2026-07-19 (fresh venv, dbos 2.27.0) and every qualitative finding reproduced identically: mid-step crash replays only the unrecorded step, completed steps never re-run, the SQLite system db has 10 tables / recovery_attempts=2, a mismatched app version leaves the workflow PENDING (orphaned), and the 200-no-op durability tax was again ~2.3ms/step. Absolute recovery latency drifts run-to-run (0.01-0.06s) and is machine-dependent; it is not a durable claim.

environment.backend: SQLite (DBOS default, zero server) — system_database_url = sqlite:///dbos-firsthand.sqlite
environment.note: This is a semantics + micro-cost probe, not a throughput benchmark. The portable signals are the RECOVERY BEHAVIOR (what replays vs re-runs) and the SHAPE of the version-trap and durability-tax, not absolute latencies.

subject.name: DBOS Transact (Python)
subject.invocation: @DBOS.workflow / @DBOS.step decorators; DBOS.launch() recovers pending workflows from the system DB on start
subject.freshness_note: 2.27.0 was 3 days old at run time; 2.28.0 alphas ship near-daily. Durable execution as a PATTERN is not new (Temporal 2019, Azure Durable Functions earlier). What is fresh in 2026: AWS Lambda durable functions (2025-12, rolling out through H1 2026) mainstreamed the category, and DBOS's library-only + SQLite-default approach drops the entry barrier to a single pip install. Pin 2.27.0 — the API surface (config keys, list_workflow_steps) moves between minor versions.

baseline.name: no-op function calls (durability tax denominator)
baseline.invocation: plain python calls vs the same calls wrapped as @DBOS.step

method.ground_truth: An append-only side-effect ledger file (ledger.txt). Each step appends 'stepN EXECUTED pid=...' BEFORE any checkpoint, so the ledger records every real (re-)execution of the code — this is what distinguishes 'the step ran again' from 'DBOS replayed a recorded output without running it'.
method.hard_kill: os._exit(42/43) — an immediate process death that skips even python finally blocks, simulating SIGKILL / power loss rather than a graceful shutdown.
method.isolation: Each experiment uses its own work dir (w-baseline / w-during / w-between / w-trap / w-overhead) and a fixed workflow id (kill-test-1), so runs never contaminate each other.
method.determinism: The workflow is deterministic by construction: fixed step count, no RNG, no clock reads inside steps.

determinism.note: WHICH steps replay vs re-run, the version-trap PENDING outcome, and the SQLite table set are deterministic and reproduce exactly. Recovery LATENCY (seconds from relaunch to SUCCESS) and the exact ms/step vary with disk + scheduler and are reported as observations, not portable numbers.

raw_artifacts.retention_policy: venv + per-experiment SQLite files + ledgers deleted after the run (finite local disk)
raw_artifacts.note: run.sh regenerates all of them deterministically from probe.py. The 2026-07-17 ledger evidence is quoted verbatim in results.json -> evidence_ledgers.
